msgs.text:
I treated the prior investigations as a proposal log, not as proof. The problem’s required syntax is the four-generator tame format with distinguished sigma,tau, implicit tau^sigma=tau^2, later generators in the pro-2 core, and exponents reduced modulo M=85667662080.The prior log leaves only the “dynamic tame-inertia” word alive; the earlier scalar, plain-π2, and fixed-τ−2 candidates were refuted by finite-tame or index-two subgroup tests.This is also consistent with the literature boundary: Neukirch describes the Jannsen–Wingberg proof as a Demuškin-formation argument over finite normal tame subextensions, including the finite tame action, while later summaries note that the p=2, K=Q2 case is not covered by Diekert’s dyadic result.Numdam+2 Tel Aviv University Math Department+2

The current candidate remains variables sigma,tau,x,y definitions nx = (x*tau^50932630783)^40491355905 ny = (y*tau)^40491355905 relations x*sigma^45176306175*x*sigma^40491355905*x^-1*nx*ny^sigma*y^-1

Equivalently, with π=π2 and η=2π on odd tame inertia,r=xσ−πxσπx−1(xτ−η)π ((yτ)π)σy−1,where π≡40491355905,−π≡45176306175,−η≡50932630783(modM).The 2-primary coordinate of η is irrelevant here because η is used only as an exponent of the prime-to-2 tame generator τ.The main audit result this turn is that the last several prior turns oscillated between reciprocal formulas. The correct formulas are as follows.For the sign-cover calculation, let V=kerκ, let t be the deck involution, and work on a nontrivial tame-inertia character block with τ↦α=1. ForNa (u)=(uτa)π,the Fox coefficient isDa=j=0∑π−1(tαa)j=1−tαa 1−(tαa)π=1−tαa1−t.

This is the only direction that makes sense: 1−tαa is a unit, while 1−t is in the radical of the 2-adic sign-cover group algebra. Hence Da is radical, and the coefficient fromx−1(xτ−η)π is −1+D−η, a unit. So nontrivial odd tame-inertia blocks contribute no 2-primary Smith torsion.

For the trivial tame-inertia block, with A=F2 [T]/(Tf−1) and s≡π(modf), the linear relation is(1+T−s)X+(1+T)Y=0.

If f=2a m with m odd, then s≡1(mod2a) and s≡0(modm). When f is even, s is odd, so 1+T∣1+T−s. The legitimate substitution is therefore

Y=P(T)X+Yˉ,P(T)=1+T1+T−s,not the reciprocal. With this P, the reduced multiplier is h(T)=1+P(T)+P(T−1).

For a nontrivial f-th root ζ,(1+ζ)h(ζ)=ζ−s+ζs+1.

Thus h(ζ)=0 would force ζ2s+1=1. But 2s+1 is odd and congruent to 1(modm), hence gcd(f,2s+1)=1. So the trivial block is nonsingular.The new structural point is that the remaining formation problem is not equally hard for all tame quotients. If f is odd, then the finite tame quotientΔe,f=⟨σˉ,τˉ∣τˉe=1,σˉf=1,σˉτˉσˉ−1=τˉ2⟩has odd order. In that case the filtered operator-action ambiguity disappears once the Frattini action is fixed.

Indeed, for a finitely generated pro-2 group U, the kernelAut1 (U)=ker(Aut(U)→Aut(U/ΦU))is pro-2. If ∣Δ∣ is odd, then the nonabelian cohomology setH1 (Δ,Aut1(U))is trivial by the profinite Schur–Zassenhaus argument, applied through finite 2-group quotients. Therefore two Δ-actions on U with the same action on U/ΦU are conjugate by an automorphism acting trivially on U/ΦU.There is also no hidden obstruction from the marked element S=σf when f is odd: since S lies in a pro-2 group and f is odd, S has a unique f-th root inside its procyclic closure, so one may replace σ by σS−1/f to obtain an actual finite-order complement. Thus the Schur–Zassenhaus splitting is compatible with the marked tame quotient in the odd-f case.For f odd, s=0, and the candidate’s finite-tame checks simplify sharply:Ue,fab≅Z2ef+1⊕C2,and, for the sign kernel Ve,f=kerκ,Ve,fab≅Z22ef+1⊕C4.The nontrivial tame-character cup blocks reduce to the identity, and the trivial tame block has h=1. Thus, for odd f, the current candidate has the correct Demuškin kernel, the correct Frattini/Kummer module, the correct sign class, and—by the rigidity observation above—no additional odd-order tame-action ambiguity remains.So the true unresolved part is narrower than the prior log often suggests. The hard formation problem is concentrated in the 2-primary unramified Frobenius component, i.e. finite tame quotients with f even. That is exactly where σπ=σsS (1−s)/f contains even-root data that Schur–Zassenhaus cannot remove, and where the prior U/U3,U/U5,… filtered-action ambiguities genuinely live.

ESTABLISHED: The live dynamic candidate remains unrefuted. I fixed the two reciprocal formulas definitively:Da=1−tαa1−t,P(T)=1+T1+T−s.With these, the prior abelian and sign-kernel conclusions survive. New this turn: for finite tame quotients with f odd, the remaining operator-action ambiguity is killed by prime-to-2 rigidity; once the Frattini action is fixed, the odd-order tame action is unique up to conjugacy.

GAP: This is still not a complete presentation proof. The unresolved case is the 2-primary unramified Frobenius action for even f, including the exact filtered effect ofσπ=σsS (1−s)/f and its interaction with the dynamic tame correction τ−2π.

NEXT: Focus exclusively on even f. The next useful target is a direct comparison, in (e,f)=(1,2),(1,4),(3,2), between the candidate’s exact filtered Frobenius action and arithmetic Kummer/Witt data through U/U5; odd-order tame quotients no longer need separate Smith-form testing.[BREAKTHROUGH]
